coredata.dc:title: Multicolor-emitting carbon dots from Malus floribunda and their interaction with Caenorhabditis elegans
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, Thomas Nesakumar
Perumal, Suguna
Vinodh, Rajangam
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: The Malus floribunda fruit extract and aqueous ammonia have been employed as carbon and nitrogen resource material, respectively to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CDs exhibit excellent lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm. The N-CDs have tunable fluorescence emission with redshift from 390 to 501 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 460 nm. Finally, N-CDs have been used as a fluorescent probe to image Caenorhabditis elegans (nematode) in vivo model. It showed low cytotoxicity and remarkable performance due to its good fluorescence quantum yield (19%). Moreover, the obtained N-CDs are potential candidates for biomedicine-related studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Malus floribunda
Hydrothermal method
Carbon dots
Tunable fluorescence
Caenorhabditis elegans
In vivo imaging

This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology ( MSIT ) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) HRTEM images of obtained N-CDs (inset (a): Particle size distribution graph). (d) XRD pattern and (e) Raman spectrum of obtained N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs (inset (a): Quantitative measurements of the composition of the N-CDs). XPS high-resolution scan of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s regions of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs and inset (a) optical photographs of N-CDs solution under daylight and UV-light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) Fluorescence excitation spectrum, (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, and (d) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence microscopy images of N-CDs with (a) blue emission, (b) green emission, (c) red emission, (d) overlapping and (e) bright-field from the nematode. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Multicolor-emitting carbon dots from Malus floribunda and their interaction with Caenorhabditis elegans

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Malus floribunda fruit extract and aqueous ammonia have been employed as carbon and nitrogen resource material, respectively to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CDs exhibit excellent lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm. The N-CDs have tunable fluorescence emission with redshift from 390 to 501 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 460 nm. Finally, N-CDs have been used as a fluorescent probe to image Caenorhabditis elegans (nematode) in vivo model. It showed low cytotoxicity and remarkable performance due to its good fluorescence quantum yield (19%). Moreover, the obtained N-CDs are potential candidates for biomedicine-related studies.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanomaterials have always been at forefront of research, carbon dots (CDs) are considered to be one of the most important carbon nanomaterials and have drawn tremendous attention because of their outstanding properties, such as strong fluorescence, high photostability, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness and versatile surface chemical modification [1–7]. These unique properties give CDs numerous advantages over other optical nanoprobes in biological systems. CDs have been widely used in photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, chemical sensors, living cell imaging, fluorescent biosensing, gene and intracellular drug delivery, and so on [8–14]. As is well known for a high quantum yield of CDs is an essential factor to be appropriate for bioimaging. Nitrogen doping can effectively improve the fluorescent quantum yield and continuously increasing attention because nitrogen atoms are small in atomic size and five valence electrons available for bonding with carbon atoms [15]. Besides, nitrogen doping can effectively tune the essential properties of CDs, including their electronic properties, and surface chemical reactivities. Until now, numerous strategies have been proposed to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs)/CDs, such as laser ablation, electrochemical, acidic oxidation, alkaline hydrolysis, pyrolysis, hydrothermal, microwave and so on [16–19]. Most of the above-mentioned methods need expensive apparatus, complicated manipulation, a large amount of strong acid or alkali, which limit their realistic application [20]. Among the existing methods, the hydrothermal method is one of the most often used techniques. It is a single-step process, easy to manipulate, cost-effective, milder reaction conditions, lower energy consumption, better stability, higher production efficiency and has controlled reaction conditions [20]. Another significant factor is the selection of appropriate precursors to synthesize highly fluorescent N-CDs. In general, nitrogen-containing organic compounds (precursors) are often used to synthesis N-CDs, which limits the large-scale, economic and it is not environmentally safe to synthesis N-CDs. In recent years, some green synthetic methods have emerged using environment-friendly and renewable biomass resources as carbon sources to synthesize N-CDs have caused extensive research [21–23].

Herein, a facile one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing highly fluorescent N-CDs using Malus floribunda fruit as a natural/green precursor is reported. The resulting N-CDs exhibited a strong tunable fluorescence with a good quantum yield of 19% and is highly soluble in water without any capping agents. The N-CDs exhibits low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Based on a strong tunable fluorescence and good biocompatibility, the N-CDs can be used as a novel fluorescent nanoprobe for multicolor bioimaging in nematode as an in vivo model system.

Synthesis of N-CDs is performed using M. floribunda fruit and aqueous ammonia by a simple hydrothermal method. In a typical protocol, the procured M. floribunda fruit was grounded well in a domestic mixer with DI water and then the fruit extract (70 mL) was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL). Subsequently, 2 mL of aqueous ammonia was added to the juice, the autoclave was sealed carefully and heated at 200 °C for a period of 12 h. After the hydrothermal process, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature and the product suspension was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter (mixed cellulose esters membrane) to eliminate large particles. The obtained supernatant was collected which was denoted as N-CDs. The illustration of the synthesis procedure is shown in Scheme 1 .

High-resolution transmittance electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis was carried out to determine the shape, size, and crystallinity of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the CDs were near-spherical in morphology and well dispersed with relatively narrow size distribution. An average diameter of 4.0 nm was observed through the particle size distribution graph as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(a). The HRTEM images (Fig. 1(b) and 1(c)), indicated that CDs are exhibited with well-resolved lattice fringes. The lattice spacing distances are approximately 0.21 nm which corresponds to the (1 0 0) plane lattice of graphite [24] and lattice fringes indicated that CDs are graphitic structures. Further, the degree of graphitization of N-CDs was resolved by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman analyses. The XRD profile in Fig. 1(d) displays strong and weak reflections centered around 25.1 and 42.5° which belongs to (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials, respectively [25]. The (0 0 2) lattice spacing distance was approximately 0.35 nm, which is slightly larger than that of bulk graphite (0.34 nm). The larger lattice distance may be attributed to nitrogen-doping and the introduction of numerous oxygen-containing groups in/on the carbon framework of N-CDs. The broad diffraction peak width is due to the small size of the N-CDs [15]. The XRD results suggest the acceptable crystallinity/graphitization of N-CDs with d-spacing of 0.21 nm which agrees well with that the (1 0 0) facet of graphite. In Raman spectrum (Fig. 1(e)) of N-CDs, the two distinct broad bands around 1351 and 1597 cm−1 ascribed to D-band (disordered carbon) and G-band (graphitic carbon) arose from the in-plane of sp3 -carbons and sp2 -hybridised carbons, respectively [26,27]. Hence, the intensity ratio of the disordered D-band and graphitic G-band (ID/IG) is a reliable indicator that verifies the changes from sp2 carbons into defective forms of sp3 configuration in graphitic structure [28]. Raman spectrum of N-CDs shows that the ID/IG is about 0.81. This value is higher than that of the typical graphene, indicating the surface of the obtained N-CDs is rich in functional groups, and result in a moderate degree of graphitization [27].

Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are the powerful tools used in observing the composition of the surface functional groups on the N-CDs. ATR-FTIR spectrum of N-CDs is shown in Fig. S1 indicates that oxygen-contained and nitrogen-contained molecules were possibly formed with carbonyl, amine, amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups in the N-CDs (See ESI for details discussion). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)) shows a predominant graphitic C 1s peak around 284 eV, an obvious N 1s peak around 400 eV and a strong O 1s peak around 532 eV. Quantitative determination by XPS shows that the synthesized N-CDs have consisted of carbon (70%), nitrogen (9%), and oxygen (21%) in atomic percentage, demonstrating the successful introduction of nitrogen and oxygen in/on the N-CDs. The prominent graphitic C 1s core level peak (Fig. 2(b)) can be deconvoluted into five different peaks centered at 284.1, 284.7, 285.8, 288.2 and 290.4 eV are ascribed to CC, CC, COH, CNC/COC, and OCOH bonds, respectively. This indicating that the synthesized N-CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl on their surface [29], which is consistent with the corresponding ATR-FTIR analysis. The N 1s level peak (Fig. 2(c)) can be resolved into three components centered at 398.4, 399.8 and 400.2 eV arising from pyridinic-N (CNC), pyrrolic-N (CNH) and graphitic-N (NC3), respectively [30,31]. This confirms that nitrogen has been doped into the N-CDs. The O 1s level peak (Fig. 2(d)) can be deconvoluted into four components centered around 531.1, 532.2, 533.2 and 535.6 eV are assigned to CO and COH/COC, OCOH and HOH groups, respectively [31,32]. According to the aforementioned characterization, the existence of remarkably higher amounts of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the N-CDs surface is confirmed. These oxygen-containing functional groups would help N-CDs to be well dispersed/soluble in an aqueous system, which is enabling them to be widely used for biochemical applications including cell imaging and drug delivery in bio-systems.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were used to investigate the optical properties of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), a typical UV–vis absorption strong peak around 285 nm and a weak shoulder peak around 325 nm corresponding to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC bond) and n → π* transition of sp3 hybrid regions (CO/NO bond), respectively [33]. This suggested the presence of amino and carbonyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, which can lead to strong fluorescence [29,33]. The N-CDs aqueous solution exhibit excellent solubility with a yellow, transparent solution. Further, it shows bright blue-cyan emission/fluorescence under UV-light (365 nm), the optical photos are shown as an inset of Fig. 3(a). As shown in Fig. 3(b), the maximum excitation wavelength observed between 330 and 340 nm for the maximum emission. As displayed in Fig. 3(c), the emission wavelength increasing from 390 to 501 nm when the excitation wavelength is shifted from 300 nm to 460 nm. This indicates that the N-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which is considered to be related to surface defects, size differences and trap states with different energy levels [34–38]. The maximum emission peak is observed around 406 nm under the excitation of 335 nm and the full width at half maximum is about 95 nm, indicating relatively uniform particles. Furthermore, excitation-dependent emission behavior elucidates through normalized excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 3(d). The fluorescent quantum yield of 19% is measured using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1 M sulfuric acid) as a reference. The good quantum yield/strong fluorescence of N-CDs may be credited to the quantum confinement of the surface energy traps. The quantum yield of N-CDs was correlated with literature data as shown in Table S1 (see ESI). The N-CDs are very stable under UV-light (365 nm) illumination (Fig. S2), the fluorescence signal almost remains constant, which facilitates their further application [39]. The digital photographs of aqueous N-CDs solution (inset Fig. S2) under UV-light (365 nm), this strong illumination supports the fluorescence stability of the N-CDs. Further, the N-CDs aqueous solution was stored for a long time and measured the fluorescence intensity at different storage times (0–120 days). No obvious aggregation or change in fluorescence intensity was observed even after storage for several months (Fig. S3), suggesting that the N-CDs exhibits stable fluorescence for a long time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of the N-CDs in nematode in vivo models
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N-CDs was used as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging in living cells. The live-cell imaging application of synthesized N-CDs was first critically evaluated the biocompatibility on the cell line. For this, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs has systematically evaluated using Caenorhabditis elegans (nematode) in vivo cell model. Fig. S4 shows the cytotoxicity graphs of N-CDs with different concentrations and different incubation time using standard established protocols with nematode [40]. While increasing the concentration of N-CDs, the gradual decrement in the cell viability was observed and an insignificant decrement in the cell viability by increasing the incubation time was observed. In fact, N-CDs yielded cell viability inhibition efficiencies of 100, 99, 99, 98, 96, 96, 95, 94 and 93% for the N-CDs concentration of 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 and 200 μg mL−1, respectively. This result explored that the N-CDs did not induce nematode death and are tolerable even at high concentration (200 μg mL−1) and for longer incubation time (48 h), which means N-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility. The maximum usable concentration of cell-based bioimaging is found to be 100 μg mL−1 and hence used for multicolor fluorescent imaging. The comparative cytotoxicity was determined by presuming untreated control owing to 100% viability. These results demonstrated that the synthesized N-CDs could serve as safe fluorescent agents for imaging of cells without any cytotoxicity and can be used for in vivo model applications. In order to investigate the potential of N-CDs for imaging, nematode was incubated with the N-CDs (100 μg mL−1) for 24 h, followed by imaging was done using a fluorescence microscope. The nematode emitted blue, green and red fluorescence upon excitation at a laser excitation of 400, 470 and 550 nm, respectively (Fig. 4 ). The multicolor emissions were clearly observed due to the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of N-CDs. The merged images revealed that the fluorescence emission of the N-CDs originated from the whole body of the nematode. The efficient fluorescence imaging might be attributed to the small size of N-CDs which had a better chance to enter into cells. Also, it was observed that there were no morphological changes in cells after incubation with N-CDs. Considering the above observations, it is clear that synthesized N-CDs may eventually be used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging and labeling for targetted drug delivery in the short future.

In summary, the fluorescent N-CDs was synthesized from Malus floribunda fruits. The obtained N-CDs are near-spherical in shape with the average diameters of ~3.5 nm and have excellent lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of 0.21 nm. The N-CDs exhibit strong and stable fluorescence with good quantum yield (19%). The remarkable multicolor fluorescence emission, excellent stability and low toxicity of N-CDs were served as a tunable fluorescent probe in the biological system and the nematode used as an in vivo model system of fluorescent bioimaging. Moreover, the cost-effective, and efficient N-CDs displayed immense potential practical applications in clinical and biological fields without sample pre-treatment.

Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison: Methodology, Resources, Validation. Suguna Perumal: Writing - review & editing. Rajangam Vinodh: Formal analysis, Software. Yong Rok Lee: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.127153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Int. J. Pharm.
